For the default case, it does initialize RPi.GPIO with the BCM mode when `gpio=None` is supplied.

But when supplying a specific GPIO module to the spi class, we shouldn't enforce it to be BCM.... Say I have an existing application, which I've coded up to already use RPi.GPIO with BOARD mode, in order to make use of it I would need to pass in `gpio=RPi.GPIO`, and then supply `gpio_DC=<board pin>`, `gpio_RST=<board pin>`

Or in the case of orange Pi, I would initialize like:

```python
import OPi.GPIO as GPIO
from luma.core.serial import spi

GPIO.setmode(GPIO.SUNXI)

spi = serial(gpio=GPIO, gpio_DC="PA01", gpio_RST="PG06", ...)
```

Right now, the above code would not be able to work.

The `setmode` method has just moved, and would get initialized once per newly created `spi` class, or in the case of when `gpio=GPIO` is supplied it is at the end users discretion to set the mode.

I'm trying to plan ahead. When you're designing public APIs it pays to consider how flexible the solution is.

What happens when the next feature request comes in to set a default for another option? Do we add another keyword parameter? How long can that go on for? Do we change the meaning of the existing parameter and break backwards compatibility?

I'd prefer to have something general. That way, when the next person says "I need to be able to set socket option X", I can just tell them to use the existing API, rather than continue changing my code.

tl;dr - both are correct. 

For now, '%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ' will be our advertised format of accepting UTC date-times. 

The '%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S' is an implementation detail. I'm using it to specify the datetime, and then am explicitly setting the timezone('UTC') on the datetime (L126).

Eventually, once I implement yaml validation against a schema (https://b.corp.google.com/issues/64951979#comment2), I'll throw an error when users do _not_ specify their string in the '%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ' format.

Should there be a way to reinstall an already installed profile?
The `continue` here makes it impossible to reinstall a profile which was already installed.
It is also not possible to uninstall a profile, since `portal_setup` does not support uninstalling (only quickinstaller does).

I'm not sure what we want to do. I see these options:

1. Do not change the behavior: we are simply not (yet) supporting reinstalling profiles.
2. Remove the installed check: we do not care whether the profile is already installed; the user should know what he's doing (+1)
3. Add a `--force` argument for changing the behavior (-1)

If we keep the current behavior (1.), I'd not only ask `portal_setup` but also `portal_quickInstaller`, so that it is possible to uninstall a product in quickinstaller (manually) and then reinstall it here. See the method in the [`gatherer`](https://github.com/4teamwork/ftw.upgrade/blob/master/ftw/upgrade/gatherer.py#L214-L228) (and #55).

If not, the best option I can think of is to generate a random vector in _ncut_relabel and use that to set eigsh's v0 argument. 

Should eigsh be modified to set a random initial residual vector itself? I probably wouldn't have realized np.random.seed wasn't fixing the output if I hadn't already been looking into the stability issues. 

As a side note, ARPACK's random initial vector seems to be reproducible - when I get a bad initial vector, it's bad every time I run the code. Also, we could have instead a `PrioritySampler` that first delegates the sampling to to the `RateSampler` and if the span is sampled, it also calls `RateByServiceSampler` and annotates it.

That way, the `Tracer` calls only `sampler.sample(span)` and we don't break encapsulation.

`PrioritySampler` would looks like:
```py
class PrioritySampler(object):
    def sample(self, span):
        sampled = rate_sampler.sample(span)
        by_service_sample.sample(span) if sampled
        return sampled
```

Using a mutable default parameter is generally a bad idea in python, due to:

> Python's default arguments are evaluated once when the function is defined, not each time the function is called (like it is in say, Ruby). This means that if you use a mutable default argument and mutate it, you will and have mutated that object for all future calls to the function as well.

[more details](https://www.google.com.ar/url?sa=t&rct=j&q=&esrc=s&source=web&cd=2&cad=rja&uact=8&ved=0ahUKEwiI7uyWk4HSAhUGjJAKHRWMAGIQFggcMAE&url=http%3A%2F%2Fdocs.python-guide.org%2Fen%2Flatest%2Fwriting%2Fgotchas%2F&usg=AFQjCNF849mrqlMC96-oZkr80MSgOb_R4w)
